dasme.name: Define scope and user context
dasme.body: Sharper than product-design clarification — you're scoping a technical system. Pick ONE user segment, ONE primary pain, ONE timeline. Move on. Cap at 4 minutes.
prompts: Can I pick a specific domain to ground this?
What's the primary objective — one metric to optimize?
Should I think of this as a single service or a multi-agent system?

dasme.name: Architect the agent system
dasme.body: Won or lost here. Draw the diagram. Share screen. Use Miro, FigJam, or Slides. Every AI system has four layers — interaction, orchestration, agents, data + infrastructure. Skip orchestration and the interviewer will ask.
prompts: Does this structure make sense before I go deeper into the model layer?

dasme.name: Specify data and model layer
dasme.body: Three things interviewers push on: what data feeds the system (specific signals, not 'user data'), which models and why (memorize the selection table), and what about memory (episodic, session, semantic).
prompts: We don't always want to use an LLM when a traditional ML model will do.

dasme.name: Map metrics and monitoring
dasme.body: Three cascading layers: model metrics → UX metrics → business metrics. Say the cascade out loud and translate one metric delta into dollars.
prompts: These metrics cascade. Model quality drives user experience, which drives business outcomes.

dasme.name: Edge cases, failure modes, scale
dasme.body: Three failure modes you must always cover: model goes down (circuit breaker), latency spikes (≤30s threshold, escalate), repetitive responses or frustration (hand off). For 10x scale: name the bottleneck FIRST, then propose concrete fixes.
prompts: Wrong: 'We'd add more servers.' Right: 'Bottleneck is the LLM API at ~200ms per call. Cache top 500 patterns covering ~40% of volume, on-premise distilled voice model, shard vector DB by region.'

model_selection.use: XGBoost / Random Forest / Gradient Boosted Trees
model_selection.why: Faster, cheaper, interpretable. ~100x less than LLM per prediction. Stakeholders can audit.

model_selection.use: Learning-to-rank (LambdaMART, neural LTR) on engagement signals
model_selection.why: Trained on your own data. Better at ordering than a general LLM.

model_selection.use: Hybrid BM25 + dense embeddings with a classifier router
model_selection.why: BM25 wins on exact terms. Dense wins on intent. Pure either fails.
10ms vs 200ms+ for LLM. At scale, 100–1000x cheaper.

model_selection.task: Nuanced reasoning, conversation, synthesis across docs
model_selection.why: Only tool that can reason across unstructured context.

model_selection.task: Deterministic business rules, safety gates, compliance
model_selection.why: Auditable, business-configurable. Models can't be trusted here.
A single omniscient agent is the #1 fail pattern.

anti_patterns.title: Spending 70% on product framing
anti_patterns.fail: 20 minutes on personas, 10 left for the system. Interviewer never sees architecture.
anti_patterns.fix: Cap product framing at 15% / 4 minutes. Pick one user, one pain, then move.

anti_patterns.title: Every component is 'AI-powered'
anti_patterns.fail: AI for prediction, AI for chat, AI for routing, AI for monitoring. Zero judgment.
anti_patterns.fix: For each component: state LLM vs ML vs rules vs nothing. Explain the choice.

anti_patterns.title: The vague 'AI magic' box
anti_patterns.fail: A single box labeled 'AI' that somehow does everything.
anti_patterns.fix: Break into named agents: Analyst (XGBoost), Voice (LLM), Executor (rules + recommender).

anti_patterns.title: Never mentioning failure modes
anti_patterns.fail: Interviewer has to prompt you. Points already lost.
anti_patterns.fix: Surface failure modes WHILE drawing — 'one trade-off I want to flag here…'

anti_patterns.title: Ignoring the model-selection question
anti_patterns.fix: 'We don't always want to use an LLM when a traditional ML model will do. XGBoost for prediction. LLM for conversation.'

anti_patterns.title: The 15-minute monologue
anti_patterns.fail: Head down, no pauses, no check-ins. Interviewer checks out.
anti_patterns.fix: After each DASME phase: 'Does this make sense? Any questions before I move on?'

anti_patterns.title: Treating scale as hypothetical
anti_patterns.fix: Name the bottleneck first. Then concrete: vector DBs, on-premise, intelligent caching.

examples.metrics: Precision, Recall, F1, AUC, per-class confusion matrix
Retrieval recall@k, answer faithfulness, citation accuracy
p50 / p95 / p99 latency, throughput, cost/inference, drift
Task completion rate, time to completion, time to first value
CSAT, thumbs-up per interaction, creator FP rate
Retention of users who touched AI vs those who didn't
ACV, GMV, saved churn, conversion lift
Gross margin per interaction, cost vs human-powered baseline

guardrail_metrics.name: Latency p99
guardrail_metrics.why: Tail experience matters more than average.

safety_checklist.question: What harms are possible?
safety_checklist.body: List ≥3 concrete harm vectors. Not abstract ('misuse') — specific ('a user tricks the agent into doxxing someone by roleplaying as customer support').

safety_checklist.question: What's the safety gate?
safety_checklist.body: Which part of the architecture blocks harmful outputs? Rules engine, classifier, human review — or all three?

safety_checklist.question: What's the appeals path?
safety_checklist.body: False positives destroy trust. How does a user appeal? Who reviews?

safety_checklist.question: What's the feedback loop?
safety_checklist.body: How do new harm patterns get labeled, retrained, and deployed?

safety_checklist.question: How is it evaluated?
safety_checklist.body: Offline harm eval set, red-team exercises, real-time telemetry on harm rate.

safety_checklist.question: Who is accountable?
safety_checklist.body: Which team owns safety incidents? What's the incident response?

vibe_tools.best_for: Non-technical PMs building web apps fast. Connected Supabase/auth. Best UX for pure vibes.
Speed. Full-stack. Good for live demos when time is tight.
UI-first. Best visual polish. Perfect for pitching a feature concept.
Full-stack with real infra. Best when the interviewer probes how it runs in production.
Existing codebase to extend, or when you want full control.
